does: This paper introduces Generalized Integrated Gradients (GIG), a method for mechanistic interpretability in image models that traces information flow from input through all intermediate layers to final outputs across entire datasets. The approach decomposes model embeddings into interpretable Concept Vectors using Pointwise Feature Vectors and Effective Receptive Fields, then computes relevance scores between these concepts via GIG. The method enables systematic, dataset-wide analysis of how images are processed throughout a network rather than providing only class-specific local explanations. The authors validate both concept extraction and concept attribution through qualitative and quantitative evaluations, demonstrating that their framework can decode the exact operations occurring within vision models at different levels of abstraction.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability paradigm that has matured in NLP, where researchers have progressively moved from explaining individual predictions to discovering global, high-level concepts. While language models have benefited from layer-wise analysis and concept-based explanation methods, image models have lagged behind—remaining largely confined to class-specific saliency maps and localization techniques. The paper builds on integrated gradients and attribution methods, adapting them for systematic concept analysis. It responds to the asymmetry in interpretability maturity between vision and language domains, drawing inspiration from how transformer-based analysis and concept discovery have advanced XAI in NLP while addressing the unique architectural and representational challenges of convolutional and modern image processing architectures.
enables: This framework opens pathways for deeper understanding of what image models actually compute at each layer, potentially revealing unexpected feature hierarchies and semantic operations. The approach could enable more sophisticated model auditing for bias and spurious correlations by tracing how problematic patterns flow through networks. Future work might leverage concept attribution to improve model robustness, transfer learning, and few-shot adaptation by explicitly manipulating identified concepts. The methodology could also facilitate knowledge distillation by making student model training more concept-aligned with teachers, and enable better anomaly detection by understanding deviation from typical concept activation patterns. Ultimately, mechanistic interpretability in vision could catalyze new architectures designed around interpretable operations rather than retrofitting explanations onto black boxes.
